The best negotiators in the world will tell you that their job isn't about agreement. It's about implementation. A deal that can't be implemented is useless. Due to this undeniable fact, yes, is nothing without how. What does that mean to great negotiators? What that means is that great negotiators are implementation architects. Great negotiators dynamically and adaptively design the negotiation to gain both agreement and implementation. These people represent the outliers that don't let implementation get overlooked. Again, yes, is nothing without how. Agreement is square one. Profits don't come from agreement. They come from implementation. In the hostage world, success isn't when the hostage taker says, okay, it's when the freed hostage says thank you to your face and the hostage taker is wearing a new set of silver bracelets. Negotiations are critically about information. You get the most vital information from the other side by keeping them talking. This is what we refer to and never split the difference as verbal vomit. This greatly increases the probability that they will reveal some crucial insight. Do you have the ability to get the other side to think out loud in front of you? A powerful way to keep the conversation flowing is to ask calibrated how questions. While closed into questions only ask for a binary response, open-ended ones subtly request more explanation and in turn trigger an entire brain shift into what Daniel Kahneman refers to as slow or in-depth thinking. Unlike open-ended why questions, how questions aren't interpreted as accusatory, so your counterpart won't get defensive. The genius behind calibrated how questions is that they cause the other party to come up with solutions. When it comes time for them to make their own demands, they will take your challenges into consideration. Another way to think of how questions is as an elegant and subtle way to reject their proposal, essentially what you're telling them is no, but without the perceived negativity that follows from a refusal of their offer. Instead, a gentle how invites calibration and leaves your counterpart feeling respected because it guides them into developing a better solution. While a straightforward no might stymie the conversation or leave the other party feeling hurt, when you ask them how, you're telling them that you'd like to come to an agreement but you need their help getting to a point where you feel comfortable, thus giving them a chance to solve the problem for you and have buy into a solution that was their idea. So the rest of this course will take you on a deeper dive into guaranteeing execution on your terms through the use of calibrated how questions. You will learn how to achieve consent both with your direct counterparts and those invisible influencers behind the scenes. You'll also learn to be able to tell the difference between people who are genuinely interested and those who give counterfeit approval. Finally, we will end with some useful rules and techniques that will make it more difficult for the other party to deny your request. The methods we'll cover in this course will allow you to extend to the point of influence to include affecting their behavior away from the table.
